title: Medication Administration: Scope of Practice and Safe Delegation
subtitle: During a busy med pass, the RN leads a team with an LPN/LVN and a UAP. She must match each medication task to the correct scope of practice, apply the five rights of delegation, and keep RN-only tasks with the RN.

patient.name: Day-Shift Med Pass (RN, LPN, UAP)
patient.admitDate: Medical unit. The charge/primary RN organizes the medication pass across the team: a stable patient due for routine oral meds, a new admission needing a first-dose IV antibiotic, a patient on a heparin drip needing titration, and a patient with new pain needing a PRN decision.

vitals.temp: New abdominal pain; PRN decision needed
Tasks matched to scope; meds safe/on time
UAP gave an IV med; reaction missed

labs.v: Being busy is not an excuse.

nursesNote: Day-shift medication pass led by the RN with an LPN/LVN and a UAP. The RN matches tasks to scope: she personally gives IV push meds, titrated drips, first doses, and blood products and makes PRN judgments for new/complex symptoms; the LPN/LVN gives routine scheduled oral/SubQ/IM meds to stable patients and reinforces teaching under supervision; the UAP supports (reminders, water, vitals) and reports but does NOT administer medications. Delegation follows the five rights (task/circumstance/person/direction/supervision), the RN retains accountability, and 'being busy' never justifies delegating outside scope.
providerOrders: Match each medication task to the correct scope of practice
RN administers IV push, titrated drips, first doses, and blood products
LPN/LVN administers routine scheduled medications to stable patients under RN supervision
UAP supports (reminders, vitals, reporting) but does not administer medications
RN supervises, evaluates responses, and retains accountability

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What governs who gives which medications?

opts.text: The RN retains accountability for delegated medication tasks
IV push medications, titration, and first doses are RN responsibilities
An LPN/LVN may give many routine medications to stable patients under RN supervision
UAPs generally do not administer medications
Any team member may give any medication if the unit is busy
Assessment, evaluation, and clinical judgment about medications stay with the RN

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: How are the five rights of delegation applied?

opts.text: Delegation follows the five rights: task, circumstance, person, direction, supervision
The right TASK must match the delegate's scope and competency
An unstable or unpredictable patient's medications should not be delegated
The RN must supervise and follow up on delegated tasks
"Being busy" justifies delegating outside someone's scope
PRN decisions needing judgment for a new symptom are RN responsibilities

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order how to assign the medication tasks

opts.text: Determine each task's required scope and the patient's stability
Keep RN-only tasks (IV push, titration, first dose, assessment) with the RN
Delegate routine meds for stable patients to the LPN/LVN within scope
Assign supportive tasks (reminders, vitals, reporting) to the UAP
Supervise, follow up, and evaluate the responses; document
Give an IV push medication
Titrate a heparin or insulin drip
Give the first dose of a new antibiotic and monitor for reaction
Decide whether to give a PRN med for new abdominal pain
Administer routine scheduled oral metoprolol to a stable patient
Give a scheduled IM or SubQ medication to a stable patient
Reinforce medication teaching the RN already started
Remind a patient to take meds and bring water
Report that a patient refused or vomited a dose

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Verify each task's scope and the patient's stability before assigning
Administer the IV push and first-dose medications yourself
Direct the LPN/LVN to give routine scheduled meds to stable patients
Have the UAP support and report, not administer medications
Evaluate responses, follow up on delegated tasks, and document
The RN gives the IV push medication herself
The LPN administers routine scheduled oral meds to stable patients
The RN performs the first-dose assessment and monitors for a reaction
The UAP reminds a patient about meds and reports a refusal
The RN delegates an IV push medication to the UAP
The LPN is assigned to titrate an insulin drip
The UAP administers a patient's oral medications
The RN assumes delegating a task also transfers accountability
